description: The meta-class: the class of all classes. Every entity that is a type or category is instance_of @class.
All software. Root of the software class hierarchy. Every program that executes on a substrate is a member of this class.
Class of software licenses under which programs and source code are distributed.
Class of organizations and companies involved in software development.
Class of human individuals relevant to software history and development.
Class of data file formats used for storing and exchanging information.
Class of communication protocols specifying rules for data exchange between systems.
Class of technical specifications defining standards and interfaces.
Software that manages hardware resources and provides common services for computer programs.
A formal language used to specify instructions for a computer.
A software application for the composition, editing, formatting, and printing of documents.
A software application for the organization and analysis of data in tabular form.
A software application for creating and manipulating raster images.
A software application for creating and editing vector graphics.
A software application for writing and editing source code.
An integrated development environment providing comprehensive facilities for software development.
A software application for creating and displaying slide-based presentations.
Software that serves HTTP responses to client requests over a network.
Software for organized storage, retrieval, and management of structured data.
Software that enables asynchronous communication between services via queued messages.
Software that stores frequently accessed data in memory to reduce latency.
Software for tracking changes to files and coordinating work among multiple contributors.
Software for scheduling and executing recurring tasks at specified times.
Software that automates the compilation and assembly of source code into executable artifacts.
Software responsible for initializing the operating system and managing system services after boot.
Software that automates building, testing, and integrating code changes in a continuous integration workflow.
A large language model deployed as a conversational interface for general-purpose text interaction.
A software framework for composing and orchestrating autonomous AI agents.
An AI-powered tool that assists developers by generating, completing, or reviewing code.
A machine learning model that generates or manipulates images from textual or visual input.
A software component implementing the Language Server Protocol to provide IDE features for a programming language.
A static analysis tool that identifies errors, style violations, and suspicious constructs in source code.
A tool that automatically reformats source code to conform to a style standard.
A permissive free software license originating at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
Version 3 of the GNU General Public License, a copyleft free software license published by the Free Software Foundation in 2007.
Version 2.0 of the Apache License, a permissive free software license published by the Apache Software Foundation in 2004.
A permissive free software license derived from the original BSD license, requiring preservation of copyright notice, conditions, and disclaimer in redistributions.
A license under which the publisher retains exclusive intellectual property rights, restricting use, modification, and redistribution.
A designation indicating that a work has no copyright restrictions and may be used freely by anyone.
American multinational technology corporation headquartered in Redmond, Washington, developing software, consumer electronics, and cloud services.
American multinational technology corporation headquartered in Cupertino, California, designing consumer electronics, software, and online services.
American multinational technology corporation specializing in internet services, search, cloud computing, and software.
American multinational technology corporation that manufactures and markets computer hardware, middleware, and software, and provides hosting and consulting services.
American multinational technology corporation specializing in database software, cloud infrastructure, and enterprise software products.
American non-profit organization that supports the development of the Firefox browser and other open-source internet tools.
American non-profit corporation that supports open-source software projects including the Apache HTTP Server and numerous related projects.
American multinational technology corporation operating social media and communication platforms including Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp.
American artificial intelligence safety company that develops large language models including the Claude family.
American artificial intelligence research organization that develops large language models and AI systems including GPT and ChatGPT.
Free software mass collaboration project announced by Richard Stallman in 1983, producing a complete Unix-compatible software system.
American non-profit technology consortium founded to support the development of the Linux kernel and other open-source software.
Hungarian-American software developer known for leading development of Microsoft Word and Excel and for establishing Hungarian notation.
Finnish-American software engineer who created the Linux kernel and the Git version control system.
American computer scientist who designed and implemented the original Unix operating system and co-created the C and Go programming languages.
American computer scientist who created the C programming language and co-developed the Unix operating system at Bell Labs.
Dutch programmer who created the Python programming language and served as its principal author until 2018.
American computer programmer who created the JavaScript programming language while at Netscape in 1995.
Danish computer scientist who designed and implemented the C++ programming language at Bell Labs beginning in 1979.
American free software activist and programmer who launched the GNU Project and founded the Free Software Foundation.
A general-purpose procedural programming language developed at Bell Labs by Dennis Ritchie between 1972 and 1973.
A general-purpose programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup as an extension of C with object-oriented and generic programming features.
A general-purpose, object-oriented programming language developed by Microsoft, first released in 2000 as part of the .NET platform.
A high-level, dynamically typed, general-purpose programming language emphasizing code readability, created by Guido van Rossum in 1991.
A class-based, object-oriented programming language designed to have as few implementation dependencies as possible, first released by Sun Microsystems in 1995.
A lightweight, interpreted programming language with first-class functions, created by Brendan Eich at Netscape in 1995.
A strongly typed programming language that builds on JavaScript, developed and maintained by Microsoft, first released in 2012.
A systems programming language focused on memory safety and performance without a garbage collector, first publicly released in 2012.
A statically typed, compiled programming language designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson, first released in 2009.
A family of programming languages with a long history and a distinctive, fully parenthesized prefix notation for syntax, originating in 1960 from John McCarthy's work at MIT.
A family of proprietary graphical operating systems developed and sold by Microsoft, first released in 1985.
A Unix-based graphical operating system developed and marketed by Apple Inc., first released in 2001 as Mac OS X.
A family of open-source Unix-like operating systems based on the Linux kernel, first released by Linus Torvalds on September 17, 1991.
A family of multitasking, multiuser operating systems that derive from the original Unix system developed at Bell Labs beginning in 1969.
A Unix derivative originally developed at the University of California, Berkeley, first released in 1978.
A mobile operating system developed and marketed by Apple Inc., first released in 2007 for the iPhone.
A mobile operating system based on a modified version of the Linux kernel, developed by Google and the Open Handset Alliance, first released in 2008.
A file format for word-processing documents based on the Office Open XML standard, developed by Microsoft and first standardized in 2006.
A file format developed by Adobe in 1993 to present documents including text formatting and images in a device-independent manner.
The standard markup language for documents designed to be displayed in a web browser, first publicly described by Tim Berners-Lee in 1991.
A lightweight, text-based data interchange format derived from JavaScript object syntax, first specified in the early 2000s.
A markup language that defines a set of rules for encoding documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable, standardized by the W3C in 1998.
A human-readable data serialization language, first proposed in 2001, often used for configuration files and data exchange.
A raster graphics file format that supports lossless data compression, standardized by the W3C in 1996.
An XML-based vector image format for two-dimensional graphics, standardized by the W3C in 2001.
Mail transfer agent originating at UC Berkeley, the dominant Unix MTA of the 1980s–1990s. Notorious for its labyrinthine configuration syntax (sendmail.cf). Still in production use.
